unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit you will explore the natural world and the weather. You will learn to name places in nature — canyons, meadows and bays — and to talk about weather, from a foggy morning to a powerful hurricane. You will compare things (which storm is stronger?), describe facts about the world, and ask questions like a real scientist. Along the way you will read about weather, listen to a weather report, speak about the world around you, and write your own nature facts and poems. By the end, you will be ready to explain how weather works and how to stay safe — just like Noah and Sophia do in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few weather and nature words each day.
Work through the eight short lessons in the Grammar file, doing the practice for each.
Read the Story, “The Storm and the Science Tent”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Learners investigating weather patterns and changes in nature

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The air you take in and let out of your lungs.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I took a deep breath before I started to speak.
practiceSentences: I took a deep breath before I started to speak.
Samira took a slow breath and dived into the pool.
How long can you hold your breath underwater?
"Catch your breath before we climb again," said Father.
My breath made little clouds in the cold morning air.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - r - e - a - t - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'breath', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The clear water that comes from your skin when you are hot.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After running in the sun, I wiped the sweat from my face.
practiceSentences: After running in the sun, I wiped the sweat from my face.
Running in hot weather makes sweat form on your skin.
Does sweat really help your body stay cool?
"Drink water when you lose sweat," said the nurse.
I felt sweat on my forehead after climbing the hill.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'sweat', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small amount of water in the air or on a surface.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The grass was wet with moisture after the rain.
practiceSentences: The grass was wet with moisture after the rain.
Clouds form when moisture in the air rises and cools.
How does a cactus keep moisture in the dry desert?
"The moisture on the window is called condensation," said Mrs Ali.
I saw moisture shining on the leaves early this morning.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - o - i - s - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'moisture', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The top or outside part of something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The surface of the lake was calm and shiny.
practiceSentences: The surface of the lake was calm and shiny.
Wind made small waves on the surface of the sea.
What lives just below the surface of the soil?
"Do not skate until the surface is safe," warned Father.
I skipped a stone across the smooth surface of the river.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - r - f - a - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'surface', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The soft hair that covers some animals' bodies.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Animals use their thick fur to stay warm in snowy weather.
practiceSentences: Animals use their thick fur to stay warm in snowy weather.
The rabbit's soft fur keeps it warm through winter.
Why does a fox grow thicker fur in the cold months?
"Never pull the kitten's fur," Mother reminded my little sister.
I brushed the cat's fur until it was smooth and shiny.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'fur', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A deep valley with steep sides, often with a river at the bottom.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The canyon was much deeper than the meadow.
practiceSentences: The canyon was much deeper than the meadow.
A river carved this deep canyon over thousands of years.
Can you hear your echo across the wide canyon?
"Stand back from the canyon edge," warned the guide.
I took a photo of the canyon at sunset.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - n - y - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'canyon', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A large field of grass and flowers where animals graze.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sheep ate grass in the sunny meadow.
practiceSentences: The sheep ate grass in the sunny meadow.
Bright flowers covered the meadow in early spring.
Did you see the butterflies dancing over the meadow?
"Let the goats graze in the meadow," said Grandfather.
We had our family picnic in a green meadow.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - e - a - d - o - w
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'meadow', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A part of the sea or a lake that is partly surrounded by land.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The small boats sailed safely into the bay.
practiceSentences: The small boats sailed safely into the bay.
The fishing boats rested in the calm bay at night.
Which birds nest along the rocky side of the bay?
"The water in the bay is warm today," said Theo.
I collected shells where the bay meets the sand.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'bay', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A time of heavy rain, wind, or snow, sometimes with thunder.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The storm made loud thunder all night.
practiceSentences: The storm made loud thunder all night.
Dark clouds warned us that a storm was coming.
Did the storm knock down any trees last night?
"Stay inside until the storm passes," said Mother.
I watched the storm flash and rumble from my window.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'storm', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Small balls of ice that fall from the sky during a storm.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The hail made a loud noise when it hit the roof.
practiceSentences: The hail made a loud noise when it hit the roof.
Balls of hail bounced across the playground like marbles.
Why does hail sometimes fall during summer storms?
"Cover the seedlings before the hail comes," said Grandfather.
I picked up a piece of hail before it melted.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'hail', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very strong storm with heavy wind and rain.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The hurricane blew down many trees.
practiceSentences: The hurricane blew down many trees.
The hurricane pushed huge waves onto the shore.
How do people prepare their homes for a hurricane?
"The hurricane is moving away from the coast," said the reporter.
We stayed with relatives when the hurricane hit our town.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - u - r - r - i - c - a - n - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'hurricane', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A spinning column of wind that touches the ground and can cause damage.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The tornado moved quickly across the field.
practiceSentences: The tornado moved quickly across the field.
The tornado lifted dust and leaves high into the sky.
Where should you shelter when a tornado is near?
"A tornado can twist like a spinning top," said Mrs Ali.
I saw pictures of a tornado in my weather book.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - o - r - n - a - d - o
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'tornado', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: When the air is full of tiny water drops and you cannot see far.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It was a foggy morning, and I could barely see the trees.
practiceSentences: It was a foggy morning, and I could barely see the trees.
The foggy air hid the tops of the tall buildings.
Why do drivers use bright lights on foggy nights?
"Hold my hand; it is too foggy to see," said Mother.
Our foggy walk to school felt like a mystery adventure.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'foggy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Covered with, or full of, snow.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We built a snowman on the snowy day.
practiceSentences: We built a snowman on the snowy day.
The snowy hills sparkled under the winter sun.
What games do you play on a snowy afternoon?
"Wear your boots on the snowy path," said Father.
I left deep footprints across the snowy garden.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'snowy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A mountain that can erupt with hot lava, ash, and gas.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The volcano erupted and made a big cloud of smoke.
practiceSentences: The volcano erupted and made a big cloud of smoke.
Hot lava poured slowly down the side of the volcano.
How long has that volcano been quiet and still?
"Our model volcano will fizz and bubble," said Adam.
I built a clay volcano for the science fair.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: v - o - l - c - a - n - o
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'volcano', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To do with the sun.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We used solar power to charge the light.
practiceSentences: We used solar power to charge the light.
Solar panels on the roof turn sunlight into electricity.
Have you ever watched a solar eclipse with special glasses?
"Solar energy is clean and free," said our teacher.
My calculator is solar, so it never needs batteries.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'solar', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The reddish-brown layer that forms on metal when it gets wet.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The old bike was covered in rust after it was left in the rain.
practiceSentences: The old bike was covered in rust after it was left in the rain.
Rust slowly covered the old gate by the farm.
Why does rust form on wet metal tools?
"Oil the hinges so rust cannot spread," said Grandfather.
I scrubbed the rust off my bicycle chain.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'rust', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A soft, fuzzy growth that forms on old food or wet things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I found some mold on the bread in the kitchen.
practiceSentences: I found some mold on the bread in the kitchen.
Mold grew on the forgotten orange at the back of the fridge.
Why does mold like warm and damp places?
"Throw away bread if you see mold," said Mother.
We studied how mold spreads for our science project.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'mold', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To move around freely with no set place to go.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Lions roam across the grasslands looking for food.
practiceSentences: Lions roam across the grasslands looking for food.
Camels roam the desert searching for green plants.
Do the village goats roam freely in the hills?
"Let the chickens roam in the yard," said Grandmother.
I love to roam the beach looking for pretty shells.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'roam', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Grand or special, or to do with kings and queens.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The royal palm tree grows tall and strong near the bay.
practiceSentences: The royal palm tree grows tall and strong near the bay.
The royal garden was full of fountains and roses.
Have you read the story about the royal falcon?
"This tent looks fit for a royal family," laughed Father.
I drew a royal palace with tall golden gates.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'royal', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and describe places in nature and different kinds of weather.
Compare two things using comparative adjectives (deeper, stronger, foggier).
Say which is the greatest using superlative adjectives (the deepest, the most powerful).
Describe what is happening now using the present continuous (“The storm is coming.”).
State facts about nature using the present simple (“Solar energy comes from the sun.”).
Say what is possible using can (“A tornado can destroy houses.”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask and answer wh- questions about the weather.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say where things are using prepositions of place, and give instructions with imperatives.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain simple ways to stay safe in a storm.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: Weather Around the World
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Weather is different all around the world. In some places, the air is foggy and grey in the morning. In other places, a hot sun makes people sweat. Rain helps plants grow, because plants need moisture. A gentle storm brings rain, but a hurricane brings very strong wind. Weather can be calm, and weather can be wild. Wherever we live, we watch the sky and learn.

readings.title: A Poem: “The Foggy Morning”
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The morning air is damp and grey,
The hills have almost gone away.
So still, so quiet, soft and slow —
The whole world hides in fog below.

readings.title: The Weather Report
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Good morning! Here is today's weather. It is foggy near the lake, so please walk slowly. Later, the sun will come out, and it will be warm. Tonight, a storm may bring rain and hail. Remember: if you hear thunder, go inside and stay calm. Stay safe, and enjoy your day!”

readings.title: Two Friends at the Science Fair
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Noah: “Look at our poster! A tornado spins faster than any other storm.”
Nadia: “Yes, and a hurricane is stronger than a normal storm. The volcano is the most powerful of all!”

readings.title: The Storm and the Science Tent
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Every year, Noah looked forward to the village science fair. His school set up a big tent near the lake, where students shared their projects with the whole community. This year, Noah and his friend Sophia had planned a weather display. They wanted to teach people how storms work and how to stay safe.
On the morning of the fair, the sky already looked strange. A grey, foggy mist covered the path. “I can barely see the ground,” Sophia said. “We should walk slowly.”
Noah nodded. “It's the foggiest morning we've had all year.”
When they reached the lake, the tent was ready. Their teacher, Mr Noah, was arranging the students' posters. “Set up your display near the solar light,” he said. “The sun isn't strong today, but it will still help.”
Nadia unrolled their poster about hurricanes, volcanoes and tornadoes. “This one shows a tornado moving across a field,” she said. “It spins faster than any other storm!”
Noah added, “And this picture shows a volcano with hot lava. It is dangerous, but it is also beautiful.”
People began to arrive. Some students talked about meadows, others about the lake or the forest. A younger boy pointed at Noah's display. “What is a canyon?” he asked.
“It's a deep valley,” Noah said. “Much deeper than a meadow. A river can run through it. A canyon can be the deepest land on Earth.”
Mr Noah clapped his hands. “Time for a safety quiz! Sophia, your turn.”
Nadia stood up. “What causes a hurricane?” she asked.
A girl raised her hand. “Warm water and strong wind?”
“Correct!” Sophia smiled.
“Now Noah,” said the teacher.
Noah pointed to a picture. “Where does hail fall from?”
“From the clouds during a storm,” a student answered.
“Right,” said Noah. “And hail can damage crops, so we must always watch the weather.”
Just then, the wind picked up. The surface of the lake began to ripple. A few parents looked up at the sky.
Suddenly, a man shouted, “It's coming back! The storm from last week!”
Some children began to panic. “Don't run!” Mr Noah said firmly. “Let's all move inside the hall. Quickly, but calmly.”
As they packed their things, Noah felt something cold on his cheek. “Hail!” he said. Small balls of ice bounced on the ground. “Let's go!”
They reached the hall just as the wind began to howl outside. Mr Noah closed the door. “We're safe here,” he said.
Nadia sat down, catching her breath. “My heart is beating fast,” she said. Her shirt was wet with sweat.
“That was scary,” said Noah. “But it was also kind of exciting.”
Inside, they carried on with their weather talk. They showed how animals use thick fur to stay warm in snowy weather, and how moisture in the soil helps plants grow.
“Rust forms when metal gets wet and is left too long,” Sophia explained. “Like that old nail outside.”
“And mold grows on food when we leave it too long,” Noah added. “That's why we always cover our lunch boxes!”
One girl pointed at a chart. “What's that tall tree near the bay?” she asked.
“That's a royal palm tree,” Sophia said. “It grows near the water. It is tall and strong, like a king's tree.”
Noah smiled. “Nature is powerful,” he said, “but we can learn to understand it.”
When the storm finally passed, the sky cleared. Sunshine filled the room. “Look,” a student said. “The light is shining brightly now.”
Mr Noah smiled. “You all did well today. You learned, you taught, and you stayed calm. You showed real courage.”
Noah and Sophia packed up their project. “Even with the storm, this was the most exciting science fair ever,” Noah said.
“And we didn't even have to roam far to learn something new,” Sophia laughed.
Outside, puddles sparkled in the sun. The wind had slowed — but the lesson had stayed.

grammar.title: Comparative Adjectives
grammar.explanation: Comparative adjectives compare two things. They tell us how one thing is more or less than another — bigger, deeper, stronger. They help us describe our world clearly. For example: “A hurricane is stronger than a storm.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Short word (one syllable): add -er.  strong → stronger, deep → deeper.
Word ending in -y: change y to i, add -er.  happy → happier, foggy → foggier.
Long word (two or more syllables): use more before it.  beautiful → more beautiful.
Always use than to join the two things you compare.
Watch out for irregulars: good → better, bad → worse.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “A hurricane is strong than a storm.”  ✗
Fix: Use the comparative form and “than”: “A hurricane is stronger than a storm.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: “-er for one syllable, more for two or more!” Think of the word than as a little bridge that joins the two things you are comparing.
grammar.practice: Write the comparative form of the word in brackets. | An elephant is ______ (big) than a dog. | This test is ______ (easy) than the last one. | Mount Kenya is ______ (high) than that hill. | The canyon is ______ (deep) than the meadow.

grammar.title: Superlative Adjectives
grammar.explanation: Superlative adjectives compare three or more things. They show that something is the most or the greatest of all. For example: “The volcano is the most powerful natural event.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Short word (one syllable): add -est.  tall → tallest, deep → deepest.
Word ending in -y: change y to i, add -est.  foggy → foggiest.
Long word (two or more syllables): use most before it.  exciting → most exciting.
Always put the before a superlative:  the tallest, the foggiest.
Irregulars: good → best, bad → worst.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “This is most foggy morning.”  ✗
Fix: Add “the”, and use the right form: “This is the foggiest morning.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think “super means the best!” A superlative always needs its friend the standing in front of it.
grammar.practice: Write the superlative form of the word in brackets. (Remember “the”.) | This is ______ (funny) film I have ever seen. | She is ______ (smart) student in our class. | That was ______ (exciting) match of the season. | This is ______ (foggy) morning of the year.

grammar.title: Present Continuous
grammar.explanation: The present continuous describes an action happening right now. It makes your speaking lively: you can say exactly what is going on this moment. For example: “The deer is roaming the meadow.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the correct form of to be + verb + -ing.
I am reading.
He / She / It is roaming.   (“The storm is coming.”)
We / You / They are watching.   (“The leaves are rusting in the rain.”)
Some verbs are not usually used this way (stative verbs): know, like, understand. We say “I know the answer”, not “I am knowing”.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He is play football.”  ✗
Fix: Add -ing to the action word: “He is playing football.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Picture a photo of the action happening now — someone running or dancing. If you can see it happening, use am / is / are + -ing.
grammar.practice: Write the present continuous form of the verb in brackets. | The dog ______ (bark) at the gate. | I ______ (eat) my lunch. | They ______ (play) in the park. | The rain ______ (fall) on the roof.

grammar.title: Present Simple (Facts)
grammar.explanation: The present simple is used for facts, habits and things that are always true — like science facts. For example: “Solar energy comes from the sun.”  “Fur keeps animals warm.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For I, you, we, they → use the base verb:  “Birds fly south.”
For he, she, it → add -s or -es:  “It rains.”  “She watches the sky.”
Negatives use do not / does not:  “Ice does not stay hard in the sun.”
Questions start with do / does:  “Does hail fall from clouds?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Fur keep animals warm.”  ✗
Fix: With it (fur), add -s: “Fur keeps animals warm.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: “He, she, it — add -s to fit!” When the subject is one person or thing, the verb gets its little -s.
grammar.practice: Write the correct present simple form of the verb in brackets. | The Earth ______ (move) around the sun. | Cats ______ (chase) mice. | The sun ______ (rise) in the east. | Solar power ______ (come) from the sun.

grammar.title: The Modal Verb “can”
grammar.explanation: Can is a special helping verb. It tells us what is possible or what someone is able to do. For example: “A tornado can destroy houses.”  “You can see moisture on leaves.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Subject + can + base verb (no -s, -ed or -ing).
Yes: “I can swim.”
No: “I cannot (can't) swim.”
Question: “Can you swim?”  (Swap the order.)
Good news: can never changes. It stays “can” for I, you, he, she, it, we and they.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He cans play guitar.”  ✗    Also: “I can playing.”  ✗
Fix: “He can play guitar.”  ✓   Keep the base verb after “can”.
grammar.memoryTip: Think of can as a little superhero: it gives the verb the power to happen — and superheroes never change their name!
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with can + the verb in brackets. | I ______ (dance) very well. | They ______ (finish) their homework on time. | She ______ (see) the stars at night. | A storm ______ (damage) crops.

grammar.title: Wh- Questions + Prepositions of Place + Imperatives
grammar.explanation: Wh- questions start with a wh- word and ask for information. Good learners ask lots of them! For example: “What causes a hurricane?”  “Where does hail form?”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
The wh- words: what, where, when, why, who, how.
Order: wh- word + helping verb + subject + verb.  “Where does hail form?”
Match the word to the answer: where for a place, when for a time, why for a reason.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You are going where?”  ✗
Fix: Put the wh- word first and swap the order: “Where are you going?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Call them your “wonder words” — what, where, when, why, who and how are the keys that unlock new information about the world.
grammar.practice: Write the correct wh- word to complete each question. | ______ causes rainbows? | ______ do you go to school? | ______ is your best friend? | ______ do you feel today? | Lesson 7:  Prepositions of Place | What it means and why it matters | Prepositions of place tell us where something is. They help us describe positions clearly. For example: “The moss grows on the surface.”  “We stayed inside the hall.” | How to form it | Choose the preposition that shows the position between two things: | on — resting on a surface:  “The bird is on the roof.” | under — below something:  “The rabbit hides under a bush.” | in / inside — within a space:  “We are inside the hall.” | next to — beside:  “The lamp is next to the sofa.” | between — in the middle of two:  “The ball is between the chairs.” | A common mistake | Mistake: “The cat is in the table.”  ✗ | Fix: Use on for resting on a surface: “The cat is on the table.”  ✓ | Memory tip | Picture a map of your room. Say where each thing sits: “The bed is in the corner, the rug is under the chair.” The little location word is the preposition. | Practice 7 | Choose the correct preposition:  on · under · in · next to · between | The cat is ______ the box. (it is inside) | The toy is ______ the table. (resting on top) | The chair is ______ the desk. (beside it) | The dog is lying ______ the tree. (below the branches) | Lesson 8:  Imperatives | What it means and why it matters | Imperatives give a command, instruction or request. They tell someone what to do. For example: “Observe the storm safely!”  “Move inside!” | How to form it | Use the base verb, with no subject:  “Go.”  “Listen.”  (We do not say “You go.”) | To say “do not”, add don't:  “Don't run!” | To be polite, add please:  “Please sit down.” | To include yourself, use Let's:  “Let's move inside.” | A common mistake | Mistake: “You listen to me.”  ✗ | Fix: Drop the “you” — it is already understood: “Listen to me.”  ✓ | Memory tip | Remember: “No ‘you’, just the action!” An imperative is a friendly nudge that jumps straight to the verb. | Practice 8 | Write the correct imperative form of the verb in brackets. | ______ (close) the door, please. | ______ (not touch) that! | ______ (help) your sister with her homework. | ______ (listen) carefully to the instructions.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Describe Today's Weather
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Look outside, then get ready to say:
“Today the weather is ______.”  (foggy / sunny / rainy / windy)
“The sky looks ______.”
“It is a good day for ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you speak clearly? Did you use at least one weather word from this unit?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise describe today's weather and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Compare Two Things in Nature
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise comparing, using comparative adjectives:
“A canyon is deeper than a meadow.”
“A hurricane is stronger than a storm.”
“Today is foggier than yesterday.”
Now make three sentences of your own comparing two things you can see.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise compare two things in nature and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Guess the Weather
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person thinks of a kind of weather but keeps it secret.
The others ask questions: “Is it hot?” “Can you see far?” “Does it make a loud noise?”
Keep asking until someone guesses: “Is it a storm?” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise guess the weather and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: A Weather Fact File
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose one kind of powerful weather: a hurricane, a tornado, or a volcano. Write three or four fact sentences about it. Use the present simple for facts. For example: “A hurricane brings strong wind. It can knock down trees.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about a weather fact file.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2: Your Own Weather Poem
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write your own short poem called “The ______ Morning” (snowy, sunny, rainy, stormy). Use describing words for how it looks, sounds and feels, like the poem you read:
The morning air is ______ and ______, / The ______ have almost gone away. / ______, ______, ______ and ______ — / The whole world ______ below.
Then draw a picture to go with your poem.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about your own weather poem.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 3: Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about connected paragraph.

writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about information text.

activities.title: Activities 1: True or False (Nature Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
A canyon is deeper than a meadow.
Solar energy comes from the moon.
Hail is made of small balls of ice.
A gentle storm is stronger than a hurricane.
Answers: 1. True   2. False (it comes from the sun)   3. True   4. False (a hurricane is stronger)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Match the Word to the Meaning
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word to its meaning. Write the pairs (for example, 1–c).
1. meadow      a. small balls of ice that fall in a storm
2. hail        b. a large field of grass and flowers
3. moisture    c. the soft hair that covers some animals
4. fur         d. a small amount of water in the air
Answers: 1–b   2–a   3–d   4–c
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Fill in the Blanks (Weather Around You)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Today the weather is ______.
The kind of weather I like best is ______, because ______.
In a storm, the safest thing to do is ______.
One animal that has thick fur is the ______.
These are about you, so there is no single right answer — check your sentences with your AI tutor.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Ask a Scientist (Wh- Questions)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the wh- question for each answer.
______________________?  →  “A hurricane is caused by warm water and strong wind.”
______________________?  →  “Hail forms in the clouds during a storm.”
______________________?  →  “Solar power comes from the sun.”
Example answers: 1. What causes a hurricane?   2. Where does hail form?   3. Where does solar power come from? (Your wording may be a little different — check with your tutor.)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Nature & Weather learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1: A Weather Fact File and a recording of Speaking 1: Describe Today's Weather. Improve both pieces using the Grade 4 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'breath' mean?
quizzes.options: A small amount of water in the air or on a surface. | The air you take in and let out of your lungs. | The soft hair that covers some animals' bodies. | The top or outside part of something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The air you take in and let out of your lungs.
quizzes.explanation: I took a deep breath before I started to speak.

quizzes.question: What does 'sweat' mean?
quizzes.options: A deep valley with steep sides, often with a river at the bottom. | The clear water that comes from your skin when you are hot. | The soft hair that covers some animals' bodies. | The top or outside part of something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The clear water that comes from your skin when you are hot.
quizzes.explanation: After running in the sun, I wiped the sweat from my face.

quizzes.question: What does 'moisture' mean?
quizzes.options: A deep valley with steep sides, often with a river at the bottom. | A large field of grass and flowers where animals graze. | A small amount of water in the air or on a surface. | The soft hair that covers some animals' bodies.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A small amount of water in the air or on a surface.
quizzes.explanation: The grass was wet with moisture after the rain.

quizzes.question: What does 'surface' mean?
quizzes.options: A deep valley with steep sides, often with a river at the bottom. | A large field of grass and flowers where animals graze. | A part of the sea or a lake that is partly surrounded by land. | The top or outside part of something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The top or outside part of something.
quizzes.explanation: The surface of the lake was calm and shiny.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Storm and the Science Tent': Nadia sat down, catching her _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Nadia sat down, catching her breath.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Weather Around the World': In other places, a hot sun makes people _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: In other places, a hot sun makes people sweat.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Weather Around the World': Rain helps plants grow, because plants need _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Rain helps plants grow, because plants need moisture.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Storm and the Science Tent': The _____ of the lake began to ripple.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: The surface of the lake began to ripple.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Storm and the Science Tent': They showed how animals use thick _____ to stay warm in snowy weather, and how moisture in the soil helps plants grow.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: They showed how animals use thick fur to stay warm in snowy weather, and how moisture in the soil helps plants grow.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Storm and the Science Tent': A _____ can be the deepest land on Earth.” Mr Noah clapped his hands.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: A canyon can be the deepest land on Earth.” Mr Noah clapped his hands.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the weather report aloud so I can listen, then quiz me?”
“Can you read the poem with me and explain the describing words?”
“Can you be Noah while I am Sophia, so we can act out the science fair talk?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise describing the weather?”
“Can you play ‘Guess the Weather’ with me?”
“Can you check that my comparing sentences are correct?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my weather fact file and tell me what to fix?”
“Can you help me find describing words for my poem?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on these five words?”
“What is the difference between moisture and sweat?”
“Can you give me one more example sentence for each word?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me picture a canyon, a meadow and a bay?”
“Are there any canyons, meadows or bays in East Africa?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“What is the difference between a hurricane and a tornado?”
“How can people stay safe in a storm?”
“Does it snow where I live? What weather do we have most?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Why does metal get rust? Why does food grow mold?”
“Can you use each of these words in a new sentence for me?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise comparative adjectives about weather?”
“Why do we say ‘he likes’ but ‘I like’ in the present simple?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: The air you take in and let out of your lungs. I took a deep breath before I started to speak.
The clear water that comes from your skin when you are hot. After running in the sun, I wiped the sweat from my face.
A small amount of water in the air or on a surface. The grass was wet with moisture after the rain.
The top or outside part of something. The surface of the lake was calm and shiny.
The soft hair that covers some animals' bodies. Animals use their thick fur to stay warm in snowy weather.
A deep valley with steep sides, often with a river at the bottom. The canyon was much deeper than the meadow.
A large field of grass and flowers where animals graze. The sheep ate grass in the sunny meadow.
A part of the sea or a lake that is partly surrounded by land. The small boats sailed safely into the bay.
A time of heavy rain, wind, or snow, sometimes with thunder. The storm made loud thunder all night.
Small balls of ice that fall from the sky during a storm. The hail made a loud noise when it hit the roof.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name and describe kinds of weather and places in nature.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can compare two things (deeper, stronger, foggier).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say which is the greatest (the deepest, the most powerful).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what is happening now (“The storm is coming.”).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can state a fact about nature (“Fur keeps animals warm.”).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can ask and answer wh- questions about the weather.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
